result:
The concept of "letting go" is perhaps the most difficult human endeavor, and yet it is the very thing our lives are built upon. From the moment we are born, we are in a process of constant subtraction. We lose the warmth of the womb, the reliance of infancy, the innocence of childhood, and eventually, the people and versions of ourselves that once defined our landscape. We are essentially walking museums of what used to be, curating ghosts in the quiet galleries of our minds.

Consider the way we interact with time. We speak of time as if it were a river, something that flows past us, but that is a comforting fiction. If time were a river, we could stand on the bank and watch it go. Instead, we are the current itself. We are the movement, the water, the turbulence, and the foam. We are never the same person from one moment to the next because our cells are constantly regenerating, our perspectives are constantly shifting, and our memories are constantly being rewritten by the act of recalling them. Every time you remember an event from your childhood, you are not accessing a pristine file in a cabinet; you are recreating the memory based on who you are today. You are painting over the canvas of the past with the colors of the present. This means that we are constantly mourning versions of ourselves that never actually existed in the static, objective way we imagine they did.

There is a profound loneliness in this realization, but there is also a profound liberation. If we are not fixed entities, if we are fluid and ever-changing, then the mistakes of yesterday do not have to be the definitions of tomorrow. We tend to cling to our identities as if they were lifeboats. "I am a shy person," we say. "I am someone who doesn’t take risks." "I am the kind of person who always fails at this." These sentences are anchors, and we have spent years polishing them until they shine like armor. But armor is heavy. It protects you from the arrows of the world, but it also prevents you from feeling the wind or the rain.

I often think about the architecture of cities—specifically, how they are built on top of themselves. If you dig beneath the streets of London or Rome, you find layers of history: Roman tiles, medieval foundations, Victorian sewage systems, and modern cables. The city doesn’t stop being a city because it adds a new layer. It integrates the old into the new. We do the same. We carry the ruins of our former selves within us. The heartbreak you felt at sixteen is still buried somewhere in the basement of your psyche, even if you’ve built a modern office building on top of it. Sometimes, a loud noise or a specific smell can make that basement vibrate, and you feel that ancient grief again, sharp and sudden, as if it happened ten minutes ago. We are walking archaeological sites.

This brings me to the idea of curiosity. If we accept that we are constantly changing, the most logical response is to be curious about what comes next. Most people spend their lives trying to achieve a state of "arrival." We think, "Once I get the job, once I move to that city, once I find that partner, I will finally be *me*." We imagine a final destination where we can stop running, a place where our anxieties will vanish and we will finally be content. But life is not a journey toward a destination; it is a performance of a piece of music. You don't listen to a symphony to get to the final chord. The point of the music is the music itself while it is playing. The point of the life is the living of it while it is happening.

If we stop viewing life as a series of hurdles to clear and start viewing it as a series of experiments, the texture of our days changes. We become less afraid of failing because a failure is just data. It is a signpost that says, "Not this way." It is not an indictment of our character. Imagine if a scientist failed an experiment and decided, "Well, I guess I’m just a failure of a scientist, I’ll never touch a beaker again." We would think that was absurd. And yet, we do that to ourselves every day. We miss a deadline and decide we are lazy. We say something awkward and decide we are social pariahs. We lose a battle and decide we have lost the war.

The antidote to this self-flagellation is a sense of wonder. Look at the world around you. Even in the most mundane settings—a plastic chair in a waiting room, the hum of a refrigerator, the way the light hits a dusty window—there is an infinite amount of complexity. There is physics, biology, history, and art contained in every square inch of space. If we can widen our gaze, if we can pull ourselves out of the narrow tunnel of our own worries, we find that the world is incredibly large and our problems, while real, are often smaller than we believe them to be.

There is a Japanese concept called *wabi-sabi*, which finds beauty in imperfection, transience, and incompleteness. It celebrates the cracked tea bowl, the withered leaf, the fading light of autumn. It teaches us that things are most beautiful precisely because they are broken and temporary. In a culture that obsesses over airbrushed perfection and eternal youth, this is a revolutionary act. To embrace the crack in the bowl is to accept our own flaws as part of the narrative. It is to say, "Yes, I have been broken, and that is why I have a story."

We are not here for a long time, and the scale of our existence is microscopic compared to the vastness of the cosmos. Does that make our lives meaningless? I would argue the opposite. If we were eternal, our choices wouldn't matter. If we were invincible, our vulnerability wouldn't be precious. It is the fact that we are fragile, that our time is finite, and that our love is a gamble, that makes us beautiful. We are stardust that has gained the ability to contemplate itself. We are a part of the universe that is trying to understand the whole, and the fact that we can do that at all is a miracle of the highest order.

So, let go. Let go of the person you thought you were supposed to be. Let go of the grudges that are eating your spirit. Let go of the need to be right, to be seen, or to be finished. Trust the process of your own unfolding. Walk into the fog of the future not with a map, but with a lantern. You don't need to see the whole path; you only need to see the step in front of you. And that, I suspect, is more than enough.
